speaker: interviewer
text: --give me two minute warning? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is November 6, 1995, tape one. Phyllis Cooper Young, nee Rynska. The interviewer is Helen Desman. We're in Sunrise, Florida, USA. The language is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Helen Desman. Today is November 6, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Phyllis Cooper Young, in Sunrise, Florida, USA. We shall begin, Phyllis, by asking you to state your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Phyllis Cooper Young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden-- my maiden name first and last? Or just--?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, or--?

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. Fela, F-E-L-A, Rynska, R-Y-N-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rynska was your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birthday, Phyllis, and how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 72. I was born August 12, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a city in Poland named Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Sosnowiec for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-S-N-O-W-I-E-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-E-C?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK And what larger city was that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Kalman. And my mother's name was Frymeta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children were in your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. Could you tell me, how did the Jews in Sosnowiec earn their living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I-- being the age of I don't know, 16, 17-- I maybe began to understand, you know, a little bit more about the life. I think that in maybe only in Sosnowiec, but I think that throughout Poland, the Jewish people-- I don't think there was a middle class. You were either very comfortable or poor. Businesses, merchants, those were the people who-- who had the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had several. I can't remember them all. But one I remember, he used to make the tops to the shoes, not the whole shoe. And I don't know what happened to that. I-- I guess it didn't work out or whatever the case was. He went into some real estate. Now, when I say real estate, it's not like-- like real estate now. Somebody had a piece of land, small little piece of land, and he would, you know, negotiate with someone who wants to buy it, and that's how he would make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit about your schooling. What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school, which was-- I don't remember-- six or seven grades. It was a school for Jewish girls. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the education-- I must say, surprisingly, for a country like Poland-- it was pretty, pretty good. We had Jewish teachers. And I don't know from being here, you know, but I felt like-- for instance, we were getting some beginner's chemistry, let's say starting at fifth grade. They would take us to a lab. It wasn't a very, you know, up to date-- whatever they had in those days. And we were actually would experiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your city, in Sosnowiec-- and you'll have to excuse my pronunciation--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you say it good. You say it OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were Jewish and Gentile children separated for schooling, usually?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so. I really guess so, because that's where I went to. I know we went-- I think the boys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the boys went to yeshivas, you know. My father was orthodox. But in those days, you really wouldn't-- he was clean shaven, and that was like--

speaker: interviewer
text: A modern orthodox

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right. That's how you called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your religious home life. What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very religious. I mean, all the holidays. Shabbos, my father wouldn't write. He wouldn't handle money, went to shul. And although my sister and I were beginning to sort of like drift away a little bit from it. See, we lived like, in the suburb, like outside the city. And we would want to go get together with friends who lived in the city. We had trolley cars. Maybe I shouldn't even say it, but sometimes, she and I, we cheated, because it was a big walk. If Father had found out, he would have killed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He would have been upset at that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She would have been upset too, but I think he was more or less the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any religious training, schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheder. . When I was small child, I went to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this separate from your public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you attend cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. I guess in the steeple, when they were-- where they went to daven. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father take you and your sister to services with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because, you know, the men and the women sat separately. The only time we would go in, I think, Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are many temples or synagogues in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was synagogues. But where I lived in the outside of the city, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were these-- [? mostly these ?] steeples.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your schooling when you finished the public school system?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I wanted to go to what we called gymnasium-- high school. But you had to pay. And my father couldn't afford it. My sister on the other hand, she was brilliant. She won a scholarship. And she went to high school, gymnasium, or whatever you want to call it. I couldn't make it. I was good, and I don't mean to say it, that I want to-- I was a good student, but not good enough to get a scholarship like she got. And so I took some courses, correspondence courses, for secretarial work. But I never got a chance to even use it, because I was 16 years old when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. Let's talk a little bit more about your childhood. Were there are organizations, Jewish organizations, in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you belong to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gordoni.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gordoni was a little bit, I think, to the leftist, if I remember correctly. Zionist, but a little bit to the leftist. I just saw it mentioned in the Jewish journal. There were a lot of them. There was a Betar. That was HaShomer HaTzair, HaTzair, a lot of them. And this is actually our social life was at these--

speaker: interviewer
text: You socialized.

speaker: interviewee
text: --At these organizations, the boys and the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a goal to go to Palestine at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, from what I remember, not myself-- I was-- I was too young to even, you know, have such thoughts. But I remember that my father was planning on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emigrating to France. But he got sick. He needed surgery or some-- and the money went. Then, a few years, a couple of years before the war, I think, broke out, he was talking about Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Palestine then, of course, and they closed the quota.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would he have gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, he would've gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: In those early years, do you remember any incidents of anti-Semitism that occurred to either you, or your family, or friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was so much anti-Semitism in Poland, that one couldn't even start begin. But I'll tell you one incident. And that was during already, that was the night, the winter of 1940. I was then 17 years old. And they were predicting a snowstorm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to bed that night, and I said a prayer. I said, dear God, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] let me wake up in the morning. Let there be no snow and no Nazis. He granted me one wish, no snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you'd like to know, why snow? What's so bad about snow? Because people, kids that I used to go to school with-- I mean, I didn't go to school with, but we grew up together, next door, played, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kids don't know prejudice. Kids are taught by their parents, or racism, what they call racism today. They take a rock, and wrap the snow around it and throw it at you.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Gentile--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Gentile

speaker: interviewer
text: --neighbors of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, Polish. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the pre-war years, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the pre-war years I'm talking-- well, this was an incident. There was always anti-Semitism in Poland, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you had Gentile Christian neighbors. Did you play together with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah we played together. You know, kids get into fights. But it was always zyd, zyd, zyd, they called us. This is the Jew for-- and other words that I wouldn't even know how to translate into English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's talk some more about the pre-war years. What did you hear about what was happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not only did we hear, but we had some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] proof, so to speak. Now, you can ask me how. You see, my city that I came from, we were very close to the German border. I think we must have been about 15 miles from Auschwitz, which was Oswiecim in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was-- I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was--?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was Oswiecim in Polish. So that's the region that I came from. So when Hitler came into power-- I can't go back that far-- but I'm talking about mid '30s, already closer, maybe '37, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was getting rid of some Jews, sent them into Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come to your town? And what did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they would tells us that what's going in Germany, how they hate the Jews, and how they take away their homes, their businesses, how they literally kill them, whatever they do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know. I don't know whether we really-- well, we did believe it. Because why would people--? The German Jew-- Jews as a rule, to my knowledge, they were pretty successful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why would they just all of a sudden pick up and leave homeland, a land that they were born, their kids were-- How did the Jews in your town react to these stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: People didn't believe it. That was the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did these people, these refugees from Germany, where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They settled, like I say, in our town, the Katowice. See, that was basically-- this-- this part of Poland, I think during World War I or before, it was part of Germany. And they still, some of the state, not the Jews, but I mean, the non-Jews, they spoke German. You know, so it was easier for them to live there, because they like walked from one, you know, into something that are was easier for them to settle in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there organizations that helped them to settle? Were there Jewish groups that helped them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure there were, but the to be honest with you, I was too young to pay attention to this stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Gentile population react to what was going on in Germany? Did you notice any difference in the way they treated the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk now about the war years. Where were you on September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at home. And I happened to be up six o'clock in the morning. Was a beautiful, glorious day. The sun was shining. And I heard the plane overhead, looked up, and Hitler attacked Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you looked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the plane. I didn't see any bombing or anything like this. I heard the-- like shots, like maybe they were-- the Polish army-- that was another joke. They were, I guess, maybe trying to shoot it down, or whatever. But I ran in back into the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next? Next, we were listening to the radios. And we were told that Hitler attacked Poland. For the next couple of days, of course, we were very upset. Well, actually, we knew it was coming. Because that August, the whole month of August, that was the whole topic, you know, that whatever was happening, Hitler-- this one trying to make a deal with him. This one and that one. We knew that it's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any Jewish or were any men drafted into the Polish army at that time before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh sure. The Jewish men were drafted to the Polish, absolutely. They were in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone in your family taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because we were two girls. I don't remember any of my uncles. I think they were too old. But the next couple of days, it was like, just sitting there listening to the radio and how they were attacking, and how they were almost in Warsaw, and you know, stuff like that. People started running, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] packing up their belongings, and going-- they figured that going deeper into Poland-- to Poland. Because we were so close to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: German border

speaker: interviewee
text: And I understand. How true it is, I don't know, but I understand that when the German army started marching into Poland, especially through the region where I lived, the sympathizers-- and Polish army was retreating-- the sympathizers were throwing down hot oil on them, on the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Volksdeutsche?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Volk-- that's right, the Volksdeutsche. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time you remembered seeing a Nazi soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just getting to it, because see, people started running. Everybody, whatever they could-- the wagon or something, a push cart, or whatever, they loaded up things. And they ran to these villages and stuff. My mother, I think, said to my father-- because I told you he came from a village that was a little further from where we lived, deeper into Poland. My father said, I'm not going anywhere. If I'm going to die, I want to die in my own house. That was Friday morning that they invaded. Saturday, Sunday night, because we were already hearing on the radio that they're nearing. They were not far from us at all. We spent the whole night in an attic, the whole family. And we heard them. We didn't see them. But we heard them, because of the tanks, the trucks, and the shootings. They just-- the Nazis--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the attic with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family and some other neighbors, because we lived in an apartment building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And shooting-- we couldn't see anything, but we heard it. It seems we found out that they just-- coming into the city, they just shot whoever they saw in the streets. They didn't ask questions. They were shooting. Well, we eventually had to come down from the attic. We couldn't stay there. And then, they just went on a rampage. They lined up the Jews. And they went one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, 10, boom. Just like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shot. They shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know that they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's also a very good question. The Polish people, some of them helped them out. OK? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Listen, for them to-- in those days, to pull a man's pants down, and they knew right then and there that they're dealing with Jews. It's a very common thing. But I can't-- I mean, I don't remember exactly everything that happened after that. And they set up a government provisional, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What laws did they make that changed your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, curfew. First of all we have had a curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The time? No, I can't remember. And started with the arm bands.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color arm band did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had white with the blue star of David. It's funny, because in some places they wore the yellow star. I don't know how they determined this. If I knew that, I would-- and then they formed the Jewish committee. You know, they did forms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the judenrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, the judenrate. And, you know, life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any members of that judenrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the function of the judenrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Nazis gave them orders, what to do, what they wanted done. And they had to follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that they the judenrate helped the people in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there long enough to answer that question.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard a lot about this. I have read a lot about it. I have a book here. is the name of the book. And there's also a lot about-- some of it about my city, and the judenrate, but all this happened after I was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father able to continue his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much. Things were off, there's no question about it. Food became very scarce. And if you knew somebody, like we knew somebody that had a bakery, so we knew we would have the bread, and stuff. It was rough. There is no question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a black market in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. That's something that always exists, always existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any selections made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this time, not by me. Nothing. You see now, why I feel guilty, so guilty, because I avoided all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what happened to you? How did your life change after the Nazis occupied Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were able to get around in the street, as long as I followed the curfew, and wore my arm band. And if you came across a guy that wanted to rape you or beat you up, you had to take it and go on. I mean, but it was like, it was-- I can't describe it. You were just going on. It wasn't-- you were just going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you fill your days? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister had gotten married early. And she lived like in the city. So I would go to her, you know. We were able to walk, because it wasn't that far. I would go to her, visit with her. Friends of mine, you know, we try to stay together and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an underground or resistance group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this point. You have to understand that I was taken away so early, that I really don't have the answers to all these questions, because all this came later on. I mean, it's like, how long can we take this? I've hadn't-- when they took me away, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was no ghetto. I mean, we all lived in our own apartments. As a matter of fact, they were looking for people to work, because, you see, this is my-- how I figured this-- Hitler attacked Russia in 1941, June '41. And Hitler needed man power, because he drafted every able-bodied man. He drafted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father avoid this draft?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was too old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Too old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Too old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about religious observances? Did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the same thing in the steeple, and hoping that they don't fall in on you, you know? Like everything else was done, you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it permitted, or was it forbidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't permitted. It was forbidden, but they-- and if you couldn't do it, he did it, he prayed in the house. He'd [INAUDIBLE] in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened if they had been discovered, these people that were praying?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any shootings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I myself did not witness any shootings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then. Not then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any forms of punishment during those weeks or months that you were still in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. They had like a raid, you know. They would ride down the street, and pick up a boys, girls. But what they did with them, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they return these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some did, some didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. When were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken November 3, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was asleep, seven o'clock in the morning. I heard knocking on the door. Open up the door, there were two SS men. They said-- they asked me my name. Yes. Get dressed and come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next door neighbor, friend of mine, they knocked on her door too. It seems to me that at that time from what I heard, that see, like I said, they were employing some people. They needed man power. And who better than the Jew? All right, Phyllis--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't working. Had I been working, maybe I would have remained longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, Phyllis, we're coming to the end of the tape. We're going to take a moment to change.
